chapters.title: Rejection is familiar, but it cuts deep
chapters.text:
Rejection is the feeling of being inadequate and unwanted. Most of us bump into it as we grow up. Job interviews, play dates, early crushes, and not being picked for a sports team all carry that sting. The feeling gets sharper as we get older and meet more people.

Treating rejection as a real possibility can ease the pain it brings.

Rejection has a strong emotional effect on the person on the receiving end. It sparks anger, clouds judgment, wears away the sense of belonging, and lowers self-esteem.

Rejection creates four distinct psychological wounds. The first is emotional pain, which often shows up as physical pain. Studies show that the parts of the brain that register physical and emotional pain of rejection are the same. Pain lowers clear thinking and reduces the ability to make sound decisions.

The second wound is anger and aggression. Rejection stirs those feelings, and even the gentlest people can lash out at the source. The third wound is a loss of self-esteem. Rejection can leave you feeling unworthy of human connection or love. That loss shows up as a drop in confidence, and it can affect work ethic and performance.

The fourth wound is a lack of sense of belonging. The pain of rejection grows out of our need to belong to a group, a person, an idea, or a philosophy. Once we are rejected, that sense of belonging breaks, and the pain follows.

To treat the pain, we need counter-arguments. Instead of concluding that we are the problem, we should consider that the reason for the rejection may have been out of our control. Then we can move on, whether the rejection came at work, in a relationship, or in a social setting. Look for reasons to interpret the sting. It is not always about you.

chapters.title: Loneliness is a strange feeling in a crowded room
chapters.text:
Loneliness is a kind of depression that grows from being alone or feeling cut off from the connections we want. It is the hollow feeling that is left when we lose a person or group that mattered to us.

In today's world, we can reach many people with the click of a button or the swipe of a screen, yet loneliness is on the rise. Being lonely is not the same as being alone, and many people do live alone. About 27 percent of US households are made up of a single person. There are also plenty of lonely people who live with spouses or family.

Loneliness is not about the number of human connections, but about the subjective quality of those connections. A person can have a strong circle of friends and still long for a romantic relationship, and feel lonely as a result.

Loneliness is shaped by the quality of your connections, not the count of them.

Loneliness can lead to suicidal thoughts, clinical depression, hostility, and poor sleep. It can raise blood pressure and cholesterol, and it can weaken immunity through the release of stress hormones.

Loneliness is contagious. People who spend time with lonely individuals are said to develop loneliness themselves. Even with that risk, loneliness is mostly overlooked today.

The mental health effects of loneliness are many, but three main areas take the most damage. The first is painful misconceptions. When we feel lonely, we tend to slip into a cycle of introspection, blaming ourselves for being left out. The second is self-defeating prophecies. Loneliness can make us paint false pictures of the future, where we end up alone and it is our fault. The third is atrophied relationship muscles. Once we decide loneliness is our fault, we stop trying to build new connections.

To treat the psychological wounds of loneliness, we have to fight pessimism first. Pessimism is the belief that bad things will happen no matter what. Here, it shows up as the idea that we will end up lonely no matter how hard we try. That mindset has to be challenged and replaced.

We must be willing to shift our mindset to overcome loneliness.

chapters.title: Trauma and loss often travel together
chapters.text:
Trauma is an emotional wound that leads to psychological injury. It often comes from a deeply damaging event, sometimes physical, but the aftershock is mostly mental.

Loss can lead to trauma, especially when what is lost is very dear to us. Trauma and loss are placed together here because they share a tight bond, each feeding the other in an endless cycle.

Trauma and loss are conjoined twins. To heal from trauma, you have to make peace with what was lost.

Trauma from loss has four main effects. The first is an interrupted life. Trauma can stop a person from sliding back into a normal routine. Even after the physical pain is gone, the emotional weight can keep everyday life out of reach.

The second is an interrupted identity. Trauma can erase a person's sense of self. They move through life unsure of who they are or what they are here for. The third is interrupted beliefs. During trauma, faith in oneself can crumble, and depression can set in.

The fourth is an interrupted relationship. Some trauma is tied to relationships, especially for people who have been in many painful ones. It can become hard to form new bonds.

There are steps to take to treat the wounds of loss and trauma. They have to be deliberate, because loss and trauma are deeper than most emotional injuries.

First, soothe the emotional pain. The pain that comes with trauma belongs to you, and so does the job of healing. Be your own therapist and give yourself closure.

If you are traumatized, do not pile more emotional strain on yourself. Learn to heal from the trauma rather than add to it.

Second, recover the parts of yourself that were shut off by trauma and pain. Finally, try your best to find meaning in tragedy. Giving sense to what you have been through can bring the closure you need.

chapters.title: Guilt can weigh you down if it lingers
chapters.text:
Guilt is the feeling of responsibility for a wrong act, the awareness that you did something you should not have done. That aftertaste of wrongdoing is a sign of a healthy conscience. Guilt is common, because we all slip up from time to time. It can be heavy, and it keeps judging you long after the act.

Studies estimate that people feel about two hours of mild guilt a day, around five hours of moderate guilt a week, and three and a half hours of severe guilt a month. In some cases, guilty feelings can stick around for years, even decades. Guilt reminds us that we did something wrong. It is proof of an active conscience.

Guilt can work like a moral compass, or at least a reminder when we slip.

When guilt lingers, it can cause real psychological damage over time. The first kind of damage is self-condemnation. Guilt makes you overly critical of yourself, often pushing harsh judgments on your sense of worth. It can become self-loathing, where you constantly bash yourself for your mistakes.

The second kind is blocked relationships. Guilt makes it hard to connect with other people, because you are always judging yourself. There is little room left for friends, family, or partners in a mind filled with guilt.

To treat the wounds of unhealthy guilt, start by talking to yourself the way you would talk to someone you love. When you feel you have wronged another person, practice a real apology. Do not just breeze over it. Make it thoughtful. Name what you think you did wrong. Acknowledge the other person's hurt.

A proper apology keeps the smell of guilt from hanging around after the wrong has been fixed.

It also helps to forgive yourself. We are not above hurting or offending ourselves, and we have to be ready to forgive ourselves too. If we cannot forgive ourselves, we will not grow, and we will not be free of guilt.

A useful fact: the brain itself does not feel pain. It interprets pain, but the brain does not have pain-sensitive nerves. The structures around the brain are the parts that feel pain. Once inside the brain, surgeons can operate without anesthesia.

chapters.title: Deep thinking can help, but rumination can harm
chapters.text:
Rumination is the act of deep thought, negative cyclic thinking, persistent and recurrent worrying or brooding. It is normal to be caught up in thought. Reflective thinking is one way humans make sound decisions. But that kind of thinking can twist into something dark that nags at you without stopping.

Sometimes, we think for too long and too deeply about the wrong things, and it ends up hurting us.

Rumination is strangely destructive and can cause lasting damage if it is left to run. The effects of rumination include exaggeration of misery. When we focus too much on our problems, we can turn small hills into mountains.

Another effect is anger inflation. Every emotional response gets bigger and louder because we spend too much time thinking about it. A third effect is cognitive leak. Ruminating eats up so much mental energy that there is little left for important work. A fourth effect is strained relationships with family and loved ones. People lost in rumination often pull away from the people who care about them, because they are too caught up in their own thoughts.

The first step in treating the wounds of rumination is to shift your perspective. Instead of chewing on the negatives, try to fill your mind with positive thoughts. If that does not work, it may be time to stop the cycle entirely. Find a distraction that pulls you out of the loop for good.

Feed your mind with more positive thoughts and a kinder view of yourself and your situation.

chapters.title: Failure haunts even the most confident minds
chapters.text:
Failure is a state where you do not meet a goal you wanted to reach. It leaves a sour taste, especially when you put in real effort.

Failure is common, but it still hurts because it touches our unfulfilled dreams.

Failure can pull even the most skilled people into self-pity. It can keep them from reaching their full potential. It does not have to be a massive failure. Small failures can pile up and break the will to try again.

Fear of failure is a strong enemy, and even without that fear, failure can still sting. Some of the psychological effects of failure include reduced self-esteem, where the constant replay of failure leaves little room for confidence.

Another effect is passivity and helplessness. Letting failure consume you can make you feel stuck. A third effect is performance pressure. Once failure has taken hold, you begin to expect to fail in everything.

To handle the effects of failure, start by being kind to yourself. Instead of harsh self-judgment, shore up your confidence and look for lessons in what went wrong.

Everyone makes mistakes. What differs is how and what we learn from them.

Another way to treat the wounds of failure is to focus on what you can control. It is more useful to put your energy there. Stop worrying or blaming yourself for what you cannot control, and direct your effort toward what you can change.

It also helps to take responsibility for your failures. We are not perfect. We will make mistakes. Accept that, and use the slip as a prompt to do better next time. As you accept that you will sometimes fail, look for ways to step back from the pressure of always performing at a peak level. Approach each task without telling yourself you must ace it on the first try. You will find some peace in the freedom from the strain of failure.

As Guy Winch reminds us, we often ignore our psychological wounds until they are bad enough to get in the way of daily life.

chapters.title: Low self-worth grows from self-destructive habits
chapters.text:
Low self-esteem is the result of a steady drumbeat of rejection, long stretches of loneliness, slow recovery from loss and trauma, guilt that has been nursed on purpose, and runaway rumination.

A person dealing with rejection will feel their self-esteem wear away. Low self-esteem is dangerous, because it can leave even the most talented people feeling helpless.

Low self-esteem is like a fast car with no fuel in the tank. It can run fast on a good day, but not today.

The psychological damage of low self-esteem cuts deeper than most other wounds. It feeds the doubts you have about yourself and your abilities, and it slowly wears away whatever confidence is left.

Among the wounds that low self-esteem inflicts, the most common are a weakened ego, an inability to accept positive remarks, and disempowerment. A weakened ego means your confidence dips, you lose your sense of self, and your self-assurance takes a hit.

An inability to accept positive remarks means even a kind word feels uncomfortable. Disempowerment often shows up as a sense of weakness, both physical and emotional.

This condition is not terminal. It can be treated well if you are determined to get better. One way to treat the wounds of low self-esteem is to practice self-compassion. Do not be so hard on yourself. Cut yourself some slack. Celebrate the small wins.

Celebrate the small wins. They are the building blocks of future success.

Second, instead of dwelling on your weaknesses and failures, name your strengths and affirm them. Build on them as you go. Finally, remember that compliments are not there to mock you. They are useful feedback that you really do deserve. You are already doing a good job. Do not deny yourself the satisfaction of accepting that, especially when others say it out loud. Accept it. It will do you a world of good.

chapters.title: Final thoughts on healing emotional wounds
chapters.text:
It is no secret that we will face many situations that leave us scarred and changed, but we have to find a way to heal and move on. Without a trained physician, we have to tend to our physical wounds ourselves. That is what first aid is: a patch until we can get real help. For every emotional wound covered in this book, there is a quick fix aimed right at it. Take charge of your life today. Do not let an emotional wound keep you stuck for good.

The first step in healing emotional pain is to forgive yourself and go easy on yourself. It is not your fault that you were rejected, and it is not your fault that other experiences have brought you down. When we hold on to the belief that we are not worthy of acceptance, our self-esteem rots from the inside. Be aware that the thoughts you keep feeding yourself can either build your confidence or quietly destroy it.

Guilt is normal when you have misbehaved, but when you let it stew and linger, you are only hurting yourself, in body and mind. Emotional pain can register as strongly as physical pain, and it can make it hard to fit back into daily life. It should not be brushed off. Acknowledge the link between your mental and physical health today.

As Guy Winch notes, we skip the voting booth when we do not believe the candidate of our choice can win.

A simple way to start is to make time at the end of each day to be calm and still your mind. Meditation is not easy for everyone, but a few quiet minutes can help clear the day's worries.
